content: A radioactive element having half-life 30 min . is undergoing beta decay. The fraction of radioactive element remains undecayed after 90 min . will be
explanation:
We use the formula:

$$ \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{N}_0}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{t}{T}} $$

Here, $ t = 90 $ min (total time passed), and $ T = 30 $ min (half-life).

So, $ \frac{t}{T} = \frac{90}{30} = 3 $

Substitute this in the formula: $$ \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{N}_0} = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3 = \frac{1}{8} $$

This means after 90 minutes, $\frac{1}{8}$ of the radioactive element is still left, or not decayed.

content: The ratio of the total energy of the $2^{\text {nd }}$ orbit electron for the hydrogen atom ($^1\mathrm{H}$) to that of a helium ion ($\mathrm{He}^+$) is :
explanation:
Step 1: Recall energy formula for hydrogen-like species

For a hydrogen-like atom/ion (nuclear charge $Z$):

$ E_n = - \frac{13.6\, Z^2}{n^2}\ \text{eV} $

Step 2: Energy of electron in H atom ($Z=1$, $n=2$)

$ E_{\text{H}, n=2} = -\frac{13.6 (1)^2}{2^2} = -\frac{13.6}{4} = -3.4\ \text{eV} $

Step 3: Energy of electron in He$^+$ ion ($Z=2$, $n=2$)

$ E_{\text{He}^+, n=2} = -\frac{13.6 (2)^2}{2^2} = -\frac{13.6 \times 4}{4} = -13.6\ \text{eV} $

Step 4: Ratio

We are asked for the ratio of the total energies (taking the values as they are, not just magnitudes unless specified).

$ \frac{E_{\text{H}, n=2}}{E_{\text{He}^+, n=2}} = \frac{-3.4}{-13.6} = \frac{1}{4} $

✅ Final Answer:

Option D: $\tfrac{1}{4}$

content: The magnetic moment of electron due to orbital motion is proportional to ( $\mathrm{n}=$ principal quantum number)
explanation:
Step 1. Recall expression for orbital magnetic moment

An electron in the $n$th Bohr orbit has current loop behavior. The orbital magnetic moment is

$ \mu = \frac{e}{2m} L, $

where $L$ is the orbital angular momentum.

Step 2. Angular momentum in Bohr model

From Bohr’s quantization condition:

$ L_n = n \hbar. $

So,

$ \mu_n = \frac{e}{2m} (n\hbar). $

Step 3. Relation to Bohr magneton

$ \mu_B = \frac{e \hbar}{2m}. $

So,

$ \mu_n = n \mu_B. $

Answer:

The orbital magnetic moment is proportional to $n$.

✅ Correct option: A. $n$

content: The frequencies for series limit of Balmer and Paschen series are ' $\mathrm{V}_1$ ' and ' $\mathrm{V}_3$ ' respectively. If frequency of first line of Balmer series ' $\mathrm{V}_2$ ' then the relation between $V_1, V_2$ and $V_3$ is
explanation:
$$ v=R Z^2\left(\frac{1}{n^2}-\frac{1}{m^2}\right) $$

Series limit of Balmer: $\mathrm{n}=2, \mathrm{~m}=\infty$

$$ \therefore \mathrm{v}_1=\frac{\mathrm{RZ}^2}{4} $$

Series limit of Paschen: $\mathrm{n}=3, \mathrm{~m}=\infty$

$$ \therefore \quad v_2=\frac{R Z^2}{9} $$

$1^{\text {st }}$ line of Balmer series: $\mathrm{n}_1=2, \mathrm{n}_2=3$

$$ \therefore \quad \mathrm{v}_3=\mathrm{RZ}^2\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{RZ}^2}{4}-\frac{\mathrm{RZ}^2}{9}=\mathrm{v}_1-\mathrm{v}_2 $$

content: A radio active element has rate of disintegration 8000 disintegrations per minute at a particular instant. After four minutes it becomes 2000 disintegrations per minute. The decay constant per minute is
explanation:
Given:

  • Initial rate of disintegration: $R_0 = 8000$ disintegrations/minute

  • Rate after $t = 4$ minutes: $R = 2000$

Decay law for activity $R$:

$ R = R_0 e^{-\lambda t} $

So,

$ \frac{R}{R_0} = e^{-\lambda t} $

$ \frac{2000}{8000} = e^{- \lambda (4)} $

$ \frac{1}{4} = e^{-4\lambda} $

Take logarithm:

$ -4\lambda = \ln\left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right) = -\ln 4 $

$ \lambda = \frac{\ln 4}{4} $

Now,

$ \ln 4 = \ln (2^2) = 2 \ln 2 $

So,

$ \lambda = \frac{2 \ln 2}{4} = \frac{1}{2} \ln 2 = 0.5 \ln 2 $

✅ Correct Option: C. $0.5 \log_e 2$

content: In Paschen series, wavelength of first line is ' $\lambda_1$ ' and for Brackett series, wavelength of first line is ' $\lambda_2$ ' then ratio $\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}$ is
explanation:
Using Rydberg's formula,

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right) $$

For Paschen series, $\mathrm{n}_1=3$ and $\mathrm{n}_2=4$

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R\left(\frac{1}{9}-\frac{1}{16}\right)=\frac{7 R}{16 \times 9} $$

For Brackett series,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{n}_1=4 \text { and } \mathrm{n}_2=5 \\ & \frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{16}-\frac{1}{25}\right)=\frac{9 \mathrm{R}}{16 \times 25} \\ & \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{9 \mathrm{R}}{16 \times 25} \times \frac{16 \times 9}{7 \mathrm{R}}=\frac{81}{175} \end{aligned} $$

content: The ratio of energies of photons produced due to transition of electron of hydrogen atom from its (i) third to $2^{\text {nd }}$ energy level and (ii) highest energy level to $3^{\text {rd }}$ level is
explanation:
Case (i): Electron transition from $n=3 \to n=2$.

Photon energy:

$ E = 13.6 \left(\frac{1}{n_2^2} - \frac{1}{n_1^2}\right)\ \text{eV}. $

So,

$ E_{(3 \to 2)} = 13.6 \left(\frac{1}{2^2} - \frac{1}{3^2}\right). $

$ = 13.6 \left(\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{9}\right). $

$ = 13.6 \left(\frac{9 - 4}{36}\right). $

$ = 13.6 \cdot \frac{5}{36}. $

$ = \frac{68}{36} = \frac{17}{9}\ \text{eV}. $

Case (ii): Transition from highest energy level ($n = \infty$) to $n=3$.

Energy of photon:

$ E = 13.6 \left(\frac{1}{3^2} - \frac{1}{\infty^2}\right). $

$ = 13.6 \cdot \frac{1}{9}. $

$ = \frac{13.6}{9} = \frac{136}{90} = \frac{68}{45}\ \text{eV}. $

Now ratio:

$ \frac{E_{(3 \to 2)}}{E_{(\infty \to 3)}} = \frac{\tfrac{17}{9}}{\tfrac{68}{45}}. $

Simplify:

$ = \frac{17}{9} \cdot \frac{45}{68}. $

$ = \frac{17 \cdot 45}{9 \cdot 68}. $

$ = \frac{17 \cdot 5}{68}. $

$ = \frac{85}{68}. $

$ = \frac{85}{68}. $

Divide numerator and denominator by 17:

$ = \frac{5}{4}. $

Final Answer:

$ \boxed{5:4} $

That matches Option B. ✅

content: In hydrogen atom in its ground state, the first Bohr orbit has radius ' $\mathrm{r}_1$ '. When the atom is raised to one of its excited states, the electrons orbital velocity becomes one-third. The radius of that orbit is
explanation:
From Bohr's theory:

Velocity \& radius in nth orbit:

$$ V_n=\frac{V_1}{n} \& r_n=r_1 n^2 $$

From given,

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_n=\frac{V_1}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{V_1}{n}=\frac{V_1}{3} \\ \therefore & n=3 \end{aligned} $$

So, the electron is in the third Bohr orbit.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & r_n=r_1 n^2=r_1(3)^2 \\ \therefore \quad & r_n=9 r_1 \end{array} $$

content: A radioactive element has rate of disintegration 9000 disintegration per minute at a particular instant. After two minutes it becomes 3000 disintegration per minute. The decay constant per minute is
explanation:
We know that, $\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda s}$

In 2 mins the activity reduces from 9000 d.p.m. to 3000 d.p.m. ,

$$ \begin{aligned} & 3000=9000 \mathrm{e}^{-2 \lambda} \\ & \frac{3000}{9000}=\mathrm{e}^{-2 \lambda} \\ & \frac{1}{3}=\mathrm{e}^{-2 \lambda} \end{aligned} $$

Taking $\log _e$ on both sides,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \log _e\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)=-2 \lambda \\ & -\log _e(3)=-2 \lambda \\ & \lambda=\frac{1}{2} \log _e(3)=0.5 \log _e 3 \end{aligned} $$

content: In hydrogen atom, transition from the state $\mathrm{n}=6$ to $n=1$ results in ultraviolet radiation. Infrared radiation will be obtained in the transition
explanation:
Step 1: Recall hydrogen energy level formula

For hydrogen:

$ E_n = - \frac{13.6 \, \text{eV}}{n^2} $

The energy of the photon emitted in a transition $n_i \to n_f$ is:

$ \Delta E = 13.6 \left(\frac{1}{n_f^2} - \frac{1}{n_i^2}\right) \, \text{eV} $

Step 2: Ultraviolet vs Visible vs Infrared

  • Large energy difference → higher frequency → shorter wavelength → ultraviolet.

  • Moderate energy difference → visible.

  • Small energy difference → longer wavelength → infrared.

Step 3: Analyze options

  • (A) $n=3 \to n=1$:

$\Delta E = 13.6 \left(1 - \frac{1}{9}\right) = 13.6 \times \frac{8}{9} \approx 12.1 \, \text{eV}$.

This corresponds to wavelength $\lambda \approx 1240/12.1 \approx 102 \,\text{nm}$.

That is deep UV. Not IR.

  • (B) $n=4 \to n=2$:

$\Delta E = 13.6 \left(\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{16}\right) = 13.6 \times \frac{3}{16} = 2.55 \,\text{eV}$.

$\lambda \approx 1240/2.55 \approx 486 \,\text{nm}$ → visible (blue-green). Not IR.

  • (C) $n=6 \to n=2$:

$\Delta E = 13.6 \left(\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{36}\right) = 13.6 \times \frac{8}{36} \approx 3.02 \,\text{eV}$.

$\lambda \approx 1240/3.02 \approx 410 \,\text{nm}$. Visible (violet). Not IR.

  • (D) $n=5 \to n=3$:

$\Delta E = 13.6 \left(\frac{1}{9} - \frac{1}{25}\right) = 13.6 \times \frac{16}{225} = 0.967 \,\text{eV}$.

$\lambda \approx 1240/0.967 \approx 1280 \,\text{nm}$. That's Infrared.

✅ Correct Answer: Option D ($n=5 \to n=3$)

content: A radioactive element ${ }_{92}^{242} \mathrm{X}$ emits two $\alpha$ particles, one electron and two positrons. The product nucleus is represented by ${ }_{\mathrm{p}}^{234} \mathrm{Y}$. The value of $P$ is
explanation:
We are given:

A nucleus $\,^{242}_{92}X\,$.

It undergoes the following emissions:

  • Two $\alpha$-particles (helium nuclei: $\,^{4}_{2}\mathrm{He}$):

  • Each $\alpha$ emission decreases the mass number $A$ by 4 and the atomic number $Z$ by 2.

  • So, two $\alpha$ particles:

    • Decrease in $A$: $2 \times 4 = 8$.

    • Decrease in $Z$: $2 \times 2 = 4$.

Thus after two $\alpha$ emissions:

$ A = 242 - 8 = 234,\quad Z = 92 - 4 = 88. $

Intermediate nucleus: $\,^{234}_{88}$.

  • One electron ($\beta^-$) emission:

  • A $\beta^-$ is emission of an electron when a neutron → proton.

  • So atomic number $Z$ increases by 1, mass number unchanged.

After this:

$ A = 234,\quad Z = 88 + 1 = 89. $

  • Two positron ($\beta^+$) emissions:

  • Each $\beta^+$ emission: proton → neutron, so atomic number $Z$ decreases by 1.

Two positrons → decrease $Z$ by 2.

After this:

$ A = 234,\quad Z = 89 - 2 = 87. $

Final nucleus: $\,^{234}_{87}Y$.

So, the value of $P$ is:

$ \boxed{87} $

Correct Option: A (87). ✅

content: The activity of radioactive sample is measured as $\mathrm{N}_0$ counts per minute at time $\mathrm{t}=0$, and $\frac{\mathrm{N}_0}{\mathrm{e}}$ counts per minute at time $\mathrm{t}=3$ minute, The activity reduces to half its value in time (in minute)
explanation:
Step 1: Write the decay law

The activity at time $t$ is given by:

$ N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t} $

where $\lambda$ is the decay constant.

Step 2: Find decay constant from given data

At $t=3$ min,

$ N(3) = \frac{N_0}{e} $

So,

$ \frac{N_0}{e} = N_0 e^{-3\lambda} $

Cancel $N_0$:

$ \frac{1}{e} = e^{-3\lambda} $

$ e^{-1} = e^{-3\lambda} $

$ -1 = -3\lambda \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \tfrac{1}{3} $

So, the decay constant is:

$ \lambda = \frac{1}{3}\,\text{per minute} $

Step 3: Find half-life

The half-life is:

$ T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} $

Substitute $\lambda = \frac{1}{3}$:

$ T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{1/3} = 3 \ln 2 $

Final Answer:

$ \boxed{3 \ln 2} $

That corresponds to Option B ✅

content: In hydrogen atom, the energy of electron in first and third orbit is ' $E_1$ ' and' $E_3$ ' respectively. If $E_3=x E_1$ then the value of $x$ will be
explanation:
For the hydrogen atom,

energy of an electron in the first orbit = $E_1$,

energy of an electron in the third orbit = $E_3$.

If $E_3 = x E_1$, find $x$.

Step 1: Formula for energy in hydrogen atom

$ E_n = - \frac{13.6 \, \text{eV}}{n^2} $

So, the energy is inversely proportional to $n^2$.

Step 2: Write $E_1$ and $E_3$

$ E_1 = -\frac{13.6}{1^2} = -13.6 \, \text{eV} $

$ E_3 = -\frac{13.6}{3^2} = -\frac{13.6}{9} = \frac{E_1}{9} $

Step 3: Express $E_3$ in terms of $E_1$

$ E_3 = \frac{1}{9} E_1 $

So:

$ x = \frac{1}{9} $

✅ Answer: Option A $\tfrac{1}{9}$

content:
A radioactive element A decays into radioactive element C by the following processes in succession.

$\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}+{ }_2 \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{e}}^4 ; \mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$Then elements
explanation:
$$ \mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{~B}+{ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4 ; \mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} $$

Let $A$ be : ${ }_z A^A$

In $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$ decay, ${ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4$ is released

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$ is alpha decay reaction

$\therefore \quad{ }_z \mathrm{~A}^{\wedge} \rightarrow{ }_{z-2} \mathrm{~B}^{\wedge-4}+{ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4$

In $\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}$ decay, $2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$is released

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}$ is $\beta^{-}$decay x action

$$ \mathrm{z}-2 \mathrm{~B}^{\mathrm{N}-4} \rightarrow{ }_{\mathrm{z}} \mathrm{C}^{\mathrm{\Lambda}-4} $$          ( $\because$ As each $\beta^{-}$decay increases Z by 1 )

Now A and C have the same atomic number but different mass numbers.

$\therefore \quad$ A and C are isotope.

content: ${ }_{88} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{a}}^{226}$ is converted into ${ }_{82} \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{b}}^{206}$ by emission of alpha ( $\alpha$ ) and beta ( $\beta$ ) particles. The number of alpha and beta particles emitted are respectively
explanation:
Ra-226 ($ ^{226}_{88}Ra $) decays to Pb-206 ($ ^{206}_{82}Pb $) by emission of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ particles. We need to find how many $\alpha$'s and $\beta$'s are emitted.

Step 1: Write data

  • Initial: $ A=226, Z=88$.

  • Final: $ A=206, Z=82$.

Step 2: Effect of alpha and beta emissions

  • An $\alpha$-particle emission: decreases mass number by 4, atomic number by 2. ($A \to A-4$, $Z \to Z-2$)

  • A $\beta^-$-particle emission: mass number unchanged, atomic number increases by 1 ($Z \to Z+1$).

Step 3: Compare initial and final mass numbers

Mass number difference: $226 - 206 = 20$.

  • Each $\alpha$ reduces mass number by 4.

  • To reduce by 20, we need $ \frac{20}{4} = 5$ $\alpha$ emissions.

So number of $\alpha = 5$.

Step 4: Effect on atomic number

After 5 $\alpha$ decays, atomic number decreases by $5 \times 2 = 10$.

Initial $Z=88$. After 5 $\alpha$, $Z=88-10=78$.

But final $Z$ must be 82.

So we must increase atomic number from 78 to 82 → increase by 4.

Each $\beta^-$ increases $Z$ by 1. Thus, 4 $\beta^-$ emissions.

Step 5: Answer

  • Number of $\alpha$: 5

  • Number of $\beta^-$: 4

So the correct answer = Option A: 5,4 ✅

content: Out of the following transitions in hydrogen atom, identify the transition which emits photons of highest frequency.
explanation:
Step 1: Recall the formula

The energy difference between two levels in the hydrogen atom is:

$ \Delta E = 13.6 \, \text{eV}\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right) $

(for $ n_2 > n_1 $).

The emitted photon frequency is directly proportional to this energy difference:

$ \nu = \frac{\Delta E}{h} $

So the larger the energy difference, the higher the frequency.

Step 2: Analyze each option

  • Option A: $ n=1 \to n=2 $

This describes excitation (absorption), not emission. Even if it did produce a photon, this is absorption, not emission.

  • Option B: $ n=2 \to n=1 $

This is emission. Energy difference:

$ \Delta E = 13.6\left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right) = 13.6\left(\frac{3}{4}\right) = 10.2 \, \text{eV} $

  • Option C: $ n=2 \to n=6 $

This would be excitation (absorption), not emission.

  • Option D: $ n=6 \to n=2 $

This is emission. Energy difference:

$ \Delta E = 13.6\left(\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{36}\right) = 13.6\left(\frac{9 - 1}{36}\right) = 13.6 \times \frac{8}{36} = 13.6 \times \frac{2}{9} \approx 3.02 \, \text{eV} $

Step 3: Compare

  • $ n=2 \to n=1 $: $ \Delta E = 10.2 \, \text{eV} $

  • $ n=6 \to n=2 $: $ \Delta E = 3.02 \, \text{eV} $

So the photon from $ n=2 \to n=1 $ has higher frequency (larger energy).

✅ Final Answer:

$ \textbf{Option B: } n=2 \to n=1 $

content: Using Bohr's quantization condition, the rotational kinetic energy in the third orbit for a diatomic molecule is ( $h=$ Planck's constant, $\mathrm{I}=$ moment of inertia of diatomic molecule)
explanation:
We are tasked to find the rotational kinetic energy in the third orbit for a diatomic molecule using Bohr's quantization condition.

Step 1: Bohr’s quantization condition for angular momentum

$ L = n \frac{h}{2\pi}, \quad n = 1,2,3,\dots $

Step 2: Rotational kinetic energy in terms of angular momentum

For a rotating rigid body (diatomic molecule modeled as a rigid rotor), the rotational kinetic energy is

$ E = \frac{L^2}{2I} $

Step 3: Substitute quantized angular momentum

$ E_n = \frac{1}{2I} \left( n \frac{h}{2\pi} \right)^2 $

$ E_n = \frac{n^2 h^2}{8 \pi^2 I} $

Step 4: Energy in the third orbit $(n = 3)$

$ E_3 = \frac{3^2 h^2}{8 \pi^2 I} = \frac{9h^2}{8 \pi^2 I} $

✅ Final Answer:

Option A:

$ \boxed{\frac{9 h^2}{8 \pi^2 I}} $

content: Which of the following transitions in hydrogen atom emit photons of highest frequency? ( $\mathrm{n}=$ principle quantum number)
explanation:
The frequency of the emitted photon is directly proportional to the energy difference between the two levels:

$ E = h\nu $

For the hydrogen atom, the energy of the $n^{\text{th}}$ level is:

$ E_n = -13.6 \frac{1}{n^2} \, \text{eV} $

The energy difference during a transition from $n_i$ to $n_f$ ($n_i > n_f$) is:

$ \Delta E = E_{n_f} - E_{n_i} = -13.6 \left( \frac{1}{n_f^2} - \frac{1}{n_i^2} \right) \text{eV} $

Here, a larger value of $|\Delta E|$ means a higher frequency photon is emitted.

Now, let us calculate $|\Delta E|$ for each option:

Option A: $\mathrm{n}=1$ to $\mathrm{n}=3$ (Actually, this is absorption, not emission, since $n$ increases.)

Option B: $\mathrm{n}=2$ to $\mathrm{n}=4$ (Also absorption.)

Option C: $\mathrm{n}=5$ to $\mathrm{n}=3$ (Emission, $n$ decreases.)

Option D: $\mathrm{n}=2$ to $\mathrm{n}=1$ (Emission.)

Let us consider only emission (from higher $n$ to lower $n$): Options C and D.

Option C: $n_i = 5$ to $n_f = 3$

$ \Delta E = -13.6 \left( \frac{1}{3^2} - \frac{1}{5^2}\right) $

$ = -13.6 \left( \frac{1}{9} - \frac{1}{25}\right) $

$ = -13.6 \left( \frac{25-9}{225} \right) $

$ = -13.6 \times \frac{16}{225} $

$ = -0.967 \text{ eV} $

Since emission, take absolute value: $0.967$ eV.

Option D: $n_i = 2$ to $n_f = 1$

$ \Delta E = -13.6 \left( \frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{2^2}\right) $

$ = -13.6 \left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right) $

$ = -13.6 \times \frac{3}{4} $

$ = -10.2 \text{ eV} $

Absolute value: $10.2$ eV.

$10.2$ eV (Option D) is much greater than $0.967$ eV (Option C).

Answer:

Option D ($n=2$ to $n=1$) emits photons of the highest frequency.

content: Two radioactive materials $A$ and $B$ having decay constant ' $7 \lambda$ ' and ' $\lambda$ ' respectively, initially have same number of nuclei. The time taken to have the ratio of number of nuclei of material B to that of A as ' e ' is
explanation:
Let the initial number of nuclei of both $A$ and $B$ be $N_0$.

The number of nuclei left after time $t$ for any radioactive material is given by:

$$ N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t} $$

For material $A$ (decay constant $= 7\lambda$):

$$ N_A = N_0 e^{-7\lambda t} $$

For material $B$ (decay constant $= \lambda$):

$$ N_B = N_0 e^{-\lambda t} $$

We are told that the ratio $\frac{N_B}{N_A} = e$ at time $t$.

So,

$ \frac{N_B}{N_A} = \frac{N_0 e^{-\lambda t}}{N_0 e^{-7\lambda t}} = \frac{e^{-\lambda t}}{e^{-7\lambda t}} $

$ \frac{N_B}{N_A} = e^{- \lambda t + 7\lambda t} = e^{6\lambda t} $

Given:

$ e^{6\lambda t} = e $

Take natural logarithm (log base $e$) on both sides:

$ 6\lambda t = 1 $

$ t = \frac{1}{6\lambda} $

Correct answer:

Option B: $\boxed{\frac{1}{6\lambda}}$

content: The ratio of angular momentum of an electron in $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ orbit of hydrogen atom to the velocity of electron in $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ orbit is proportional to
explanation:
For hydrogen atom, according to Bohr’s model:

Step 1: Angular momentum in the $n^\text{th}$ orbit

$$ L = m v_n r_n = n \frac{h}{2\pi} $$

Step 2: Velocity of electron in the $n^\text{th}$ orbit

The velocity in the $n^\text{th}$ orbit is:

$$ v_n = \frac{2\pi k e^2}{n h} $$

where $k$ is Coulomb's constant and $e$ is the electron charge.

Step 3: Ratio of angular momentum to velocity

We want:

$$ \frac{L}{v_n} $$

From above,

  • $L = n \dfrac{h}{2\pi}$

  • $v_n = \dfrac{2\pi k e^2}{n h}$

So,

$ \frac{L}{v_n} = \frac{n \frac{h}{2\pi}}{\frac{2\pi k e^2}{n h}} = n \frac{h}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{n h}{2\pi k e^2} = \frac{n^2 h^2}{4\pi^2 k e^2} $

This ratio is proportional to $n^2$.

Final Answer:

Option A: $\boxed{n^2}$

content: In hydrogen atom spectrum, when an electron jumps from second excited state to the first excited state, the wavelength of radiation emitted is ' $\lambda$ '. If the electron jumps from the third excited state to the second orbit, the wavelength of radiation emitted will be $\frac{20 \lambda}{x}$. The value of $x$ is
explanation:
According to Rydberg's formula,

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right) \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

When electron jumps from $2^{\text {nd }}$ excited state to first excited state,

$\mathrm{n}_2=3, \mathrm{n}_1=2$, we get

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right) $$

When electron jumps from $3^{\text {rd }}$ exited state to $2^{\text {nd }}$ orbit, $\mathrm{n}_2=4, \mathrm{n}_1=2, \lambda=\lambda_0$, we get:

$$ \begin{array}{rlrl} & & \frac{1}{\lambda_0} & =R\left(\frac{1}{4^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right) \\ & \therefore & \frac{\lambda_0}{\lambda} & =\frac{R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right)}{R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right)}=\frac{5}{36} \times \frac{16}{3}=\frac{20}{27} \\ & \therefore & \lambda_0 & =\frac{20}{27} \lambda \\ & \Rightarrow & x=27 \end{array} $$

content: In hydrogen spectrum, the ratio of wavelengths of the last line of Lyman series and that of the last line of Balmer series is
explanation:
Let us find the ratio step by step.

Step 1: Formula for Wavelength in Hydrogen Spectrum

The wavelength of a spectral line in the hydrogen spectrum is given by:

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda} = R Z^2 \left( \frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2} \right) $$

For hydrogen, $Z=1$, and $R$ is the Rydberg constant.

Step 2: Lyman Series

  • Lyman series: $n_1 = 1$

The last line corresponds to the transition from $n_2 = \infty$ to $n_1 = 1$.

So,

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda_L} = R \left( \frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{\infty^2} \right) = R (1 - 0) = R $$

Therefore,

$$ \lambda_L = \frac{1}{R} $$

Step 3: Balmer Series

  • Balmer series: $n_1 = 2$

The last line corresponds to the transition from $n_2 = \infty$ to $n_1 = 2$.

So,

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda_B} = R \left( \frac{1}{2^2} - \frac{1}{\infty^2} \right) = R \left( \frac{1}{4} - 0 \right) = \frac{R}{4} $$

Therefore,

$$ \lambda_B = \frac{1}{R/4} = \frac{4}{R} $$

Step 4: Ratio Calculation

The required ratio:

$$ \text{Ratio} = \frac{\lambda_L}{\lambda_B} = \frac{\frac{1}{R}}{\frac{4}{R}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 $$

Final Answer:

Option C: 0.25

content: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { For the following reaction, the particle ' } \mathrm{x} \text { ' is }{ }_6 \mathrm{C}^{11}\longrightarrow{ }_5 \mathrm{~B}^{11}+\beta+\mathrm{X} \end{aligned} $$
explanation:
Given reaction is:

$${}_6\mathrm{C}^{11} \longrightarrow {}_5\mathrm{B}^{11} + \beta + X$$

Let's break it down step-by-step:

  1. Identify $\beta$ particle:

A $\beta$ particle in nuclear reactions usually refers to a beta-minus ($\beta^-$) particle, which is an electron ($e^-$).

  1. Write nuclear equations:

A general $\beta^-$ decay reaction is:

$${}_Z^A X \rightarrow {}_{Z+1}^A Y + \beta^- + \overline{\nu}$$

Where:

  • $X$ is the parent nucleus,

  • $Y$ is the daughter nucleus,

  • $\beta^-$ is the emitted electron,

  • $\overline{\nu}$ is the anti-neutrino.

  1. Apply to given reaction:

In this reaction:

  • The atomic number decreases: from $6$ (Carbon) to $5$ (Boron)

  • This fits beta-plus ($\beta^+$) decay, not beta-minus

But the question writes $\beta$ (generic). However, with decrease in atomic number, it should be $\beta^+$ (positron), not $\beta^-$. But let's carry forward with the usual nuclear conventions, where

$${}_Z^A X \rightarrow {}_{Z-1}^A Y + \beta^+ + \nu$$

Here, the neutrino ($\nu$) is emitted along with the $\beta^+$.

  1. Determine particle 'X'

Comparing with the general reaction:

  • For beta-positive ($\beta^+$) decay,

    $${}_6^{11}\mathrm{C} \rightarrow {}_5^{11}\mathrm{B} + \beta^+ + \nu$$

Thus, $X = \nu =$ neutrino.

Final Answer:

Option B: neutrino

content: The frequency of revolution of an electron in the $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ orbit of hydrogen atom is
explanation:
According to Bohr's atomic model, the radius of the electron's orbit in the $n$th level is:

$$ r_n = \frac{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m_e Z e^2} $$

The speed of the electron in the $n$th orbit is:

$$ v_n = \frac{Z e^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 n h} $$

Finding angular frequency:

The angular frequency ($\omega$) is the speed divided by the radius:

$$ \omega = \frac{v}{r} = \frac{\left(\frac{Z e^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 n h}\right)}{\left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 n^2 h^2}{\pi m_e Z e^2}\right)} = \frac{\pi m_e e^4}{2 \varepsilon_0^2 h^3 n^3} $$

Finding frequency of revolution:

The frequency ($f$) is $\omega$ divided by $2\pi$:

$$ f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{\left(\frac{\pi m_e e^4}{2 \varepsilon_0^2 h^3 n^3}\right)}{2\pi} = \frac{m_e e^4}{4 \varepsilon_0^2 h^3 n^3} $$

Relation to $n$:

$$ \therefore \quad f \propto \frac{1}{n^3} $$

content: If ' $\lambda_1$ ' and ' $\lambda_2$ ' are the wavelengths of the first member of the Balmer and Paschen series, in hydrogen atom respectively, then the ratio of respective frequencies, $f_1 / f_2$, is
explanation:
First, let us identify the transitions for the first members of the Balmer and Paschen series in the hydrogen atom:

  • Balmer series (visible): transitions to $n_2 = 2$

  • First member: $n_1 = 3$ to $n_2 = 2$

  • Paschen series (infrared): transitions to $n_2 = 3$

  • First member: $n_1 = 4$ to $n_2 = 3$

Let wavelengths be:

  • $\lambda_1$ = Balmer, $\displaystyle 3 \rightarrow 2$

  • $\lambda_2$ = Paschen, $\displaystyle 4 \rightarrow 3$

The wavelength for a transition in the hydrogen atom is given by the Rydberg formula:

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \frac{1}{n_2^2} - \frac{1}{n_1^2} \right) $$

For the first Balmer line $(3 \to 2)$:

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda_1} = R_H \left( \frac{1}{2^2} - \frac{1}{3^2} \right) = R_H \left( \frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{9} \right) $$

Calculate:

$$ \frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{9} = \frac{9 - 4}{36} = \frac{5}{36} $$

So,

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda_1} = R_H \cdot \frac{5}{36} $$

For the first Paschen line $(4 \to 3)$:

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda_2} = R_H \left( \frac{1}{3^2} - \frac{1}{4^2} \right) = R_H \left( \frac{1}{9} - \frac{1}{16} \right) $$

Calculate:

$$ \frac{1}{9} - \frac{1}{16} = \frac{16 - 9}{144} = \frac{7}{144} $$

So,

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda_2} = R_H \cdot \frac{7}{144} $$

The frequency is given by $f = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$

Therefore,

$$ f_1 = \frac{c}{\lambda_1}, \quad f_2 = \frac{c}{\lambda_2} $$

$$ \frac{f_1}{f_2} = \frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1} $$

From above:

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda_1} = R_H \cdot \frac{5}{36} \implies \lambda_1 = \frac{36}{5 R_H} $$

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda_2} = R_H \cdot \frac{7}{144} \implies \lambda_2 = \frac{144}{7 R_H} $$

Therefore,

$ \frac{f_1}{f_2} = \frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1} = \frac{\frac{144}{7 R_H}}{\frac{36}{5 R_H}} = \frac{144}{7} \times \frac{5}{36} $

$ = \frac{144 \times 5}{7 \times 36} $

$ = \frac{720}{252} $

$ = \frac{720 \div 36}{252 \div 36} = \frac{20}{7} $

Correct answer:

$ \boxed{20:7} $

So, the answer is Option A.

content: The ratio of the wavelength of the last line of Paschen series to that of Balmer series is
explanation:
Step 1: Formula for wavelength in hydrogen spectrum (Rydberg formula)

The wavelength of a spectral line is given by:

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda} = R Z^2 \left( \frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2} \right) $$

where:

  • $R$ = Rydberg constant

  • $Z$ = atomic number (for hydrogen, $Z=1$)

  • $n_1$ = lower energy level

  • $n_2$ = higher energy level, $n_2 > n_1$

Step 2: Balmer series "last line"

Balmer series: $n_1 = 2$, $n_2 = \infty$ (last line means limit, $n_2 \to \infty$):

So, for the last line:

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda_B} = R \left( \frac{1}{2^2} - 0 \right) = R \cdot \frac{1}{4} $$

So,

$$ \lambda_B = \frac{1}{R} \times 4 = \frac{4}{R} $$

Step 3: Paschen series "last line"

Paschen series: $n_1 = 3$, $n_2 = \infty$ (last line):

So,

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda_P} = R \left( \frac{1}{3^2} - 0 \right) = R \cdot \frac{1}{9} $$

So,

$$ \lambda_P = \frac{1}{R} \times 9 = \frac{9}{R} $$

Step 4: Ratio of wavelengths

$$ \text{Ratio} = \frac{\lambda_P}{\lambda_B} = \frac{ \frac{9}{R} }{ \frac{4}{R} } = \frac{9}{4} $$

Step 5: Final Answer

Correct option: A $\boxed{\frac{9}{4}}$

content: Two radioactive substances A and B have decay constants ' $5 \lambda$ ' and ' $\lambda$ ' respectively. At $\mathrm{t}=0$, they have the same number of nuclei. The ratio of number of nuclei of $A$ to those of $B$ will be $\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}\right)^2$ after a time interval
explanation:
Number of nuclei remained after time $t$ can be written as $\mathrm{N} = \mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda t}$

$$\mathrm{N}_1=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-5 \lambda \mathrm{t}}\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

and $\mathrm{N}_2=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}}\quad\text{.... (ii)}$

Dividing equation (i) by equation (ii), we get,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{N_1}{N_2}=e^{(-5 \lambda+\lambda) t}=e^{-4 \lambda t}=\frac{1}{e^{4 \lambda \lambda}} \\ & \frac{N_1}{N_2}=\left(\frac{1}{e}\right)^2=\frac{1}{e^2} \quad\text{... [Given]}\\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{e^2}=\frac{1}{e^{4 \lambda \lambda}} \\ \therefore \quad & 2=4 \lambda t \Rightarrow t=\frac{2}{4 \lambda}=\frac{1}{2 \lambda} \end{aligned}$$

content: When an electron orbiting in hydrogen atom in its ground state jumps to higher excited state, the de-Broglie wavelength associated with it
explanation:
For an electron in the hydrogen atom, according to Bohr’s model,

$$ 2\pi r_n = n\lambda $$

where

  • $r_n$ = radius of the $n^{\text{th}}$ orbit

  • $\lambda$ = de-Broglie wavelength

  • $n$ = principal quantum number

Also, for hydrogen atom,

$$ r_n \propto n^2 $$

So,

$$ \lambda = \frac{2\pi r_n}{n} $$

Since $r_n \propto n^2$,

$$ \lambda \propto \frac{n^2}{n} = n $$

This means the de-Broglie wavelength increases with increase in orbit number $n$.

When the electron jumps from ground state ($n=1$) to a higher excited state ($n=2,3,\dots$), its de-Broglie wavelength increases.

Therefore, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option D: will increase}} $$

content: Frequency of the series limit of Balmer series of hydrogen atom of Rydberg's constant ' $R$ ' and velocity of light ' $C$ ' is
explanation:

content: Acceleration of an electron in the first Bohr's orbit is proportional to $\mathrm{m}=$ mass of electron, $\mathrm{r}=$ radius of the orbit, $\mathrm{h}=$ Planck's constant)
explanation:
The acceleration of an electron in the first Bohr's orbit can be analyzed through the centripetal acceleration formula, which is given by:

$$ a = \frac{v^2}{r} $$

where $ v $ is the tangential velocity of the electron in its orbit, and $ r $ is the radius of the orbit.

In Bohr's model, the angular momentum $ L $ of the electron is quantized and given by:

$$ L = mvr = \frac{nh}{2\pi} $$

where $ m $ is the mass of the electron, $ r $ is the radius of the orbit, $ n $ is the principal quantum number (for the first orbit, $ n = 1 $), and $ h $ is Planck's constant.

Solving for the velocity $ v $ from the angular momentum expression:

$$ v = \frac{h}{2\pi m r} $$

Substitute this back into the centripetal acceleration formula:

$$ a = \frac{v^2}{r} = \frac{\left(\frac{h}{2\pi m r}\right)^2}{r} = \frac{h^2}{4\pi^2 m^2 r^3} $$

Since the constant part $ \frac{1}{4\pi^2} $ is omitted when considering proportionality, the acceleration $ a $ is proportional to:

$$ \frac{h^2}{m^2 r^3} $$

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option B: $\frac{h^2}{m^2 r^3}$

content: In a hydrogen atom in its ground state, the first Bohr orbit has radius $r_1$. The electron's orbital speed becomes one-third when the atom is raised to one of its excited states. The radius of the orbit in that excited state is
explanation:
The radius of the Bohr orbit in a hydrogen atom is given by the equation:

$$ r_n = n^2 \cdot r_1 $$

where $ r_n $ is the radius of the orbit in the $ n $-th excited state, and $ r_1 $ is the radius of the first Bohr orbit in the ground state, which is approximately $ 5.29 \times 10^{-11} $ meters.

The orbital speed of an electron in the Bohr model is inversely proportional to the principal quantum number $ n $:

$$ v_n = \frac{v_1}{n} $$

If the electron's orbital speed becomes one-third of its speed in the ground state, we have:

$$ \frac{v_1}{n} = \frac{v_1}{3} $$

This simplifies to:

$$ n = 3 $$

Substituting $ n = 3 $ into the equation for the radius:

$$ r_n = 3^2 \cdot r_1 = 9 \cdot r_1 $$

Thus, the radius of the orbit in the excited state is:

Option C

$$ 9 \cdot r_1 $$

content: If 'T' is the half life of a radioactive substance then its instantaneous rate of change of activity is proportional to
explanation:
The instantaneous rate of change of activity of a radioactive substance is derived based on its half-life, $ T $.

Let's break it down step by step:

Activity Relationship:

$ R = -\frac{dN}{dt} = \lambda N $

Here, $ R $ is the activity, $ \lambda $ is the decay constant, and $ N $ is the number of undecayed nuclei.

Rate of Change of Activity:

$ \frac{dR}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(\lambda N) = \lambda \frac{dN}{dt} = \lambda(-\lambda N) = -\lambda^2 N $

Linking Decay Constant to Half-Life:

$ \lambda = \frac{\log_e 2}{T_{1/2}} $

This expression relates the decay constant $ \lambda $ to the half-life $ T_{1/2} $.

Substitute the Decay Constant:

$ \lambda^2 N = \frac{(\log_e 2)^2 N}{T_{1/2}^2} $

Proportionality of Rate of Change:

$ \frac{dR}{dt} \propto \frac{1}{T_{1/2}^2} $

Thus, the instantaneous rate of change of activity is inversely proportional to the square of the half-life, $ T_{1/2}^2 $.

content: When a hydrogen atom is raised from the ground state to the excited state

options.content: potential energy increases and K.E. decreases.
potential energy decreases and K.E. increases.
both K.E. and potential energy will increase.
both K.E. and potential energy decreases.

explanation:
In the (simplified) Bohr model of the hydrogen atom:

The total energy $E_n$ in the $n^\text{th}$ orbit is

$ E_n = -\frac{13.6\,\text{eV}}{n^2}. $

The kinetic energy of the electron is

$ K_n = -E_n = \frac{13.6\,\text{eV}}{n^2}. $

The potential energy of the electron is

$ U_n = 2E_n = -\frac{27.2\,\text{eV}}{n^2}. $

When the atom is excited from $n=1$ (ground state) to $n>1$ (an excited state):

$E_n$ becomes less negative (it increases toward 0).

$K_n$ decreases, because $K_n = 13.6\,\text{eV}/n^2$ gets smaller for larger $n$.

$U_n$ becomes less negative, which means $U_n$ is effectively increasing (going from a larger negative value to a smaller negative value).

Therefore, when a hydrogen atom is raised from the ground state to an excited state,

the potential energy increases (less negative),

the kinetic energy decreases.

Hence the correct option is:

$ \boxed{\text{(A) potential energy increases and K.E. decreases.}} $

content: In hydrogen atom, ratio of the shortest wavelength in the Balmer series to that in the Paschen series is
explanation:

The shortest wavelength in the Paschen series given by

Dividing equation (ii) by equation (i),

$$\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{9} \times \frac{4}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{4}{9}$$

content: The ratio of minimum wavelengths of Lyman and Balmer series will be

For shortest wavelength in Lyman series:

Dividing equation (ii) by equation (i),

$$\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_B}=\frac{\mathrm{R} / 4}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{1}{4}=0.25$$

content: Half-lives of two radioactive elements A and B are 30 minute and 60 minute respectively. Initially the samples have equal number of nuclei. After 120 minute the ratio of decayed numbers of nuclei of $B$ to that of $A$ will be
explanation:
For radioactive decay, amount of sample remaining is given by,

$$\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^\lambda\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

$\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}$ is initial sample and $\lambda$ is decay constant.

Decay constant, $\lambda=\left(\frac{t}{t_{1 / 2}}\right)=\frac{120}{t_{1 / 2}}\quad\text{.... (given)}$

For element A,

$$\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{120}{30}}=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}}{2^4}$$ ...[From(i)]

Amount of sample A decayed, $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\prime}=\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}-\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}-\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}}{2^4}=\frac{15}{16} \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{O}}\quad\text{.... (ii)}$

For element B,

$$N_B=N_0\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{120}{60}}=\frac{N_0}{2^2}$$ ...[From(i)]

Amount of sample B decayed,

$$\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{B}}{ }^{\prime}=\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}-\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}-\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}}{2^2}=\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{o}}$$

From (ii) and (iii),

$$\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}^{\prime}}}{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{B}^{\prime}}}=\frac{4}{5}$$

content: For hydrogen atom, ' $\lambda_1$ ' and ' $\lambda_2$ ' are the wavelengths corresponding to the transitions 1 and 2 respectively as shown in figure. The ratio of ' $\lambda_1$ ' and ' $\lambda_2$ ' is $\frac{x}{32}$. The value of ' $x$ ' is
explanation:
Using Rydberg's formula

$$\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right]=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right]=\frac{8}{9} \mathrm{R}$$

Similarly, $\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right]=\frac{3}{4} R$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{\frac{1}{\lambda_1}}{\frac{1}{\lambda_2}}=\frac{\frac{8}{9} \mathrm{R}}{\frac{3}{4} R} \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{27}{32} \\ \therefore \quad & x=27 \end{array}$$

content: The ratio of the radius of the first Bohr orbit to that of the second Bohr orbit of the orbital electron is
explanation:
The radius of the $ n $-th Bohr orbit for an electron in a hydrogen atom is given by:

$$ r_n = n^2 \cdot r_1 $$

where $ r_1 $ is the radius of the first Bohr orbit (also known as the Bohr radius).

For the first orbit ($ n = 1 $):

$$ r_1 = 1^2 \cdot r_1 = r_1 $$

For the second orbit ($ n = 2 $):

$$ r_2 = 2^2 \cdot r_1 = 4r_1 $$

The ratio of the radius of the first Bohr orbit to that of the second Bohr orbit is:

$$ \frac{r_1}{r_2} = \frac{r_1}{4r_1} = \frac{1}{4} $$

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option C

$$1: 4$$

content: A diatomic molecule has moment of inertia ' I ', By applying Bohr's quantization condition, its rotational energy in the $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ level is $[\mathrm{n} \geq 1]$ [h= Planck's constant]
explanation:
Bohr's quantization condition for the rotational motion of a diatomic molecule states that the angular momentum is quantized and given by:

$$ L = n \frac{h}{2\pi}, $$

where $ n $ is the quantum number (an integer), and $ h $ is Planck's constant.

The rotational energy $ E $ of a diatomic molecule in the $ n $-th level can be linked to its moment of inertia $ I $ and angular momentum $ L $ through the following expression:

$$ E = \frac{L^2}{2I}. $$

Substituting the quantized angular momentum into the expression for energy gives:

$$ E = \frac{\left(n \frac{h}{2\pi}\right)^2}{2I} = \frac{n^2 h^2}{8 \pi^2 I}. $$

Thus, the rotational energy of a diatomic molecule in the $ n $-th level is:

$$ \mathrm{n}^2\left(\frac{\mathrm{~h}^2}{8 \pi^2 \mathrm{I}}\right). $$

Therefore, the correct option is Option D.

content: In the uranium radioactive series, the initial nucleus is ${ }_{92}^{238} \mathrm{U}$ and that the final nucleus is ${ }_{82}^{206} \mathrm{~Pb}$. When uranium nucleus decays into lead, the number of $\alpha$-particles and $\beta$-particles emitted are
explanation:
To determine the number of $\alpha$-particles and $\beta$-particles emitted when uranium-238 (${}_{92}^{238}\mathrm{U}$) decays into lead-206 (${}_{82}^{206}\mathrm{Pb}$), we must first understand the changes in atomic and mass numbers during the decay process.

Calculation of $\alpha$-particles

An $\alpha$-particle is composed of 2 protons and 2 neutrons, equivalent to a helium nucleus $({}_{2}^{4}\mathrm{He})$. Emitting one $\alpha$-particle decreases the atomic number by 2 and the mass number by 4.

Initial nucleus: ${}_{92}^{238}\mathrm{U}$

Final nucleus: ${}_{82}^{206}\mathrm{Pb}$

Change in mass number:

$$ 238 - 206 = 32 $$

Since each $\alpha$-particle reduces the mass number by 4:

$$ \text{Number of } \alpha\text{-particles} = \frac{32}{4} = 8 $$

Calculation of $\beta$-particles

A $\beta$-particle decay involves the conversion of a neutron into a proton, increasing the atomic number by 1, but leaving the mass number unchanged.

    Change in atomic number:

    $$ 92 - 82 = 10 $$

    To account for the 10-unit change in atomic number caused by the emission of 8 $\alpha$-particles (which decrease the atomic number by 16), $\beta$-particles must be emitted to increase the atomic number by 6 units (since $16 - 10 = 6$).

    Hence, the number of $\beta$-particles is 6.

    Conclusion

    The decay of uranium-238 to lead-206 involves the emission of:

    8 $\alpha$-particles

    6 $\beta$-particles

    Therefore, the correct answer is Option D: $8 \alpha, 6 \beta$.

content: If ' $\lambda_1$ ' and ' $\lambda_2$ ' are the wavelengths of the first line of the Lyman and Paschen series respectively, then $\lambda_2: \lambda_1$ is
explanation:
In the hydrogen atom spectrum, the Lyman and Paschen series are defined by the electron transition levels. The Lyman series involves transitions where the final energy level is $n_1 = 1$, while the Paschen series involves transitions to $n_1 = 3$.

The wavelength of light emitted during a transition between two energy levels in a hydrogen atom can be determined using the Rydberg formula:

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda} = R_H \left( \frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2} \right) $$

where $ R_H $ is the Rydberg constant, $ n_1 $ is the lower energy level, and $ n_2 $ is the higher energy level.

For the first line of the series (the first transition):

Lyman series: $ n_1 = 1 $, $ n_2 = 2 $

Paschen series: $ n_1 = 3 $, $ n_2 = 4 $

Calculating the wavelength for the Lyman series:

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda_1} = R_H \left( \frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{2^2} \right) = R_H \left( 1 - \frac{1}{4} \right) = R_H \cdot \frac{3}{4} $$

Thus,

$$ \lambda_1 = \frac{4}{3 R_H} $$

Calculating the wavelength for the Paschen series:

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda_2} = R_H \left( \frac{1}{3^2} - \frac{1}{4^2} \right) = R_H \left( \frac{1}{9} - \frac{1}{16} \right) $$

Simplifying the above expression:

$$ \frac{1}{\lambda_2} = R_H \left( \frac{16 - 9}{144} \right) = R_H \cdot \frac{7}{144} $$

Thus,

$$ \lambda_2 = \frac{144}{7 R_H} $$

Finding the ratio $\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}$:

$$ \frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1} = \frac{\frac{144}{7 R_H}}{\frac{4}{3 R_H}} = \frac{144 \cdot 3}{7 \cdot 4} = \frac{432}{28} = \frac{108}{7} $$

Therefore, the ratio $\lambda_2 : \lambda_1$ is

$$ 108:7 $$

Thus, the correct answer is Option D: $108: 7$.

content: The radius of innermost orbit of hydrogen atom is $5.3 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}$. The radius of fourth allowed orbit of hydrogen atom is
explanation:
The radius of the orbit in a hydrogen atom is determined by the formula for the Bohr model of the hydrogen atom. The radius of the $ n $-th orbit is given by:

$$ r_n = n^2 \times r_1 $$

where $ r_1 $ is the radius of the innermost orbit (ground state, $ n=1 $) and $ n $ is the principal quantum number.

Given:

The radius of the innermost orbit (ground state) is $ r_1 = 5.3 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{m} $.

For the fourth orbit ($ n = 4 $), the radius is calculated as follows:

$$ r_4 = 4^2 \times r_1 = 16 \times 5.3 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{m} $$

Calculate $ r_4 $:

$$ r_4 = 16 \times 5.3 \times 10^{-11} = 84.8 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{m} $$

Converting meters to ångströms (where $ 1 \, \text{Å} = 10^{-10} \, \text{m} $):

$$ r_4 = 8.48 \, \text{Å} $$

Therefore, the radius of the fourth allowed orbit of the hydrogen atom is:

Option A

8.48 $$\mathop A\limits^o $$

content: In the third orbit of hydrogen atom the energy of an electron ' $E$ '. In the fifth orbit of helium $(Z=2)$ the energy of an electron will be

content: Ratio of longest wavelength corresponding to Lyman and Balmer series in hydrogen spectrum is
explanation:
Wavelength for Lyman series is,

$$\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^2}\right]$$

For the longest wavelength, $$\lambda=\lambda_{\max }$$ and $$\mathrm{n}=2$$

$$\frac{1}{\lambda_{\max (\mathrm{L})}}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right]=\frac{3}{4}$$

Wavelength for Balmer series is,

$$\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right]$$

For the longest wavelength, $$\mathrm{n}=3$$

content: Half life of radio-active element is 1600 years. The fraction of sample remains undecayed after 6400 years will be
explanation:
The half-life of a radioactive substance is the time it takes for half of the substance to decay. The remaining fraction of the original substance after a certain number of half-lives can be calculated using the formula:

$$N = N_0 \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{t}{T_{1/2}}}$$

Where:

$$N$$ is the remaining quantity of the substance,

$$N_0$$ is the initial quantity of the substance,

$$t$$ is the elapsed time,

$$T_{1/2}$$ is the half-life of the substance.

Given that the half-life $$T_{1/2}$$ is 1600 years, and we want to find the amount of substance left after 6400 years, we can use the above formula:

$$\frac{t}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{6400}{1600} = 4$$ half-lives

Therefore,

$$N = N_0 \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^4$$

$$N = N_0 \left(\frac{1}{2^4}\right)$$

$$N = N_0 \left(\frac{1}{16}\right)$$

The fraction of the original sample remaining undecayed after 6400 years is $$\frac{1}{16}$$.

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option A $$\frac{1}{16}$$

content: Frequency of the series limit of Balmer series of hydrogen atom in terms of Rydberg's constant (R) and velocity of light (c) is
explanation:

For series limit, $$\mathrm{n}_2=\infty$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right)=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)$$

Now, $$v=\frac{c}{\lambda}$$

$$\therefore \quad v=\frac{R c}{4}$$

content: If the radius of the first Bohr orbit is '$$r$$' then the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the $$4^{\text {th }}$$ orbit will be
explanation:
According to Bohr's second postulate,

$$\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}=\mathrm{mvr}_{\mathrm{n}}$$

$$\therefore$$ de-Broglie wavelength, $$\lambda_n=\frac{h}{m v}=\frac{2 \pi r_n}{n}$$

Also, $$\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{n}} \propto \mathrm{n}^2$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ The de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the $$4^{\text {th }}$$ orbit is:

$$\begin{aligned} &\lambda_4=\frac{2 \pi r_4}{4}=\frac{2 \pi \times(16 r)}{4} \\ &\therefore \quad \lambda_4 =8 \pi r \end{aligned}$$

content: Magnetic field at the centre of the hydrogen atom due to motion of electron in $$\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$$ orbit is proportional to
explanation:
The radius of the $$\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$$ Bohr orbit is, $$r_n \propto n^2$$.

The angular velocity of the electron, $$\omega_{\mathrm{n}} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^3}$$

Also, current $$I_n=\frac{q}{T_n}=\frac{q \omega_n}{2 \pi}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & I_n \propto \omega_n \propto \frac{1}{n^3} \\ & \text { Now, } B_n=\frac{\mu_0 I_n}{2 r_n} \\ \therefore \quad & B_n \propto \frac{1}{n^3} \times \frac{1}{n^2} \\ \therefore \quad & B_n \propto \frac{1}{n^5} \end{array}$$

content: An excited hydrogen atom emits a photon of wavelength $$\lambda$$ in returning to ground state. The quantum number $$n$$ of the excited state is ($$R=$$ Rydberg's constant)
explanation:
The wave number of emitted photon when electron jumps from $$n_1$$ to $$n_2$$ is given by

$$v=\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$$ .... (i)

Here, $$n_1=1, n_2=n$$

Put the value of $$n_1$$ and $$n_2$$ in Eq. (i), we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right] \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{1}{\lambda R}=\left[1-\frac{1}{n^2}\right] \Rightarrow \frac{1}{n^2}=1-\frac{1}{\lambda R} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{1}{n^2}=\frac{(\lambda R-1)}{\lambda R} \Rightarrow n^2=\frac{\lambda R}{(\lambda R-1)} \\ \Rightarrow & n=\sqrt{\frac{\lambda R}{(\lambda R-1)}} \end{array}$$

content:

content: In Balmer series, wavelength of the $$2^{\text {nd }}$$ line is '$$\lambda_1$$' and for Paschen series, wavelength of the $$1^{\text {st }}$$ line is '$$\lambda_2$$', then the ratio '$$\lambda_1$$' to '$$\lambda_2$$' is
explanation:
For spectral series, $$\frac{1}{\lambda}=R Z^2\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right)$$

For the Balmer series, $$\mathrm{n}_1=2$$

The wavelength for $$2^{\text {nd }}$$ line of the Balmer series is

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\mathrm{RZ}^2\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right) \\ & \frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\mathrm{RZ}^2\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{16}\right) \\ & \frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\mathrm{RZ}^2\left(\frac{3}{16}\right) \Rightarrow \lambda_1=\left[\frac{16}{3}\right] \end{aligned}$$

For the Paschen series, $$\mathrm{n}_1=3$$

The wavelength for $$1^{\text {st }}$$ line of the Paschen series is

content: In Lyman series, series limit of wavelength is $$\lambda_1$$. The wavelength of first line of Lyman series is $$\lambda_2$$ and in Balmer series, the series limit of wavelength is $$\lambda_3$$. Then the relation between $$\lambda_1$$, $$\lambda_2$$ and $$\lambda_3$$ is
explanation:
According to Rydberg's formula,

$$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~m}^2}\right)$$

For series limit of Lyman series,

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{n}=1, \mathrm{~m}=\infty, \lambda=\lambda_1 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\mathrm{R} \end{aligned}$$

For $$1^{\text {st }}$$ line of Lyman series,

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{n}=1, \mathrm{~m}=2, \lambda=\lambda_2 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{4} \end{aligned}$$

For series limit of Balmer series,

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{n}=2, \mathrm{~m}=\infty, \lambda=\lambda_3 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{\lambda_3}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{4} \end{aligned}$$

Now, $$\frac{1}{\lambda_1}-\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\mathrm{R}-\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{4}$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_1}-\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{1}{\lambda_3}$$

content: The wavelength of radiation emitted is '$$\lambda_0$$' when an electron jumps from the second excited state to the first excited state of hydrogen atom. If the electron jumps from the third excited state to the second orbit of the hydrogen atom, the wavelength of the radiation emitted will be $$\frac{20}{x} \lambda_0$$. The value of $$x$$ is
explanation:
According to Rydberg's formula,

$$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right)$$ ...... (i)

When electron jumps from $$2^{\text {nd }}$$ exited state to first exited state,

$$\mathrm{n}_2=3, \mathrm{n}_1=2, \lambda=\lambda_0$$, we get

$$\frac{1}{\lambda_0}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right)$$

When electron jumps from $$3^{\text {rd }}$$ exited state to $$2^{\text {nd }}$$ orbit,

$$\mathrm{n}_2=4, \mathrm{n}_1=2$$, we get

$$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{4^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \frac{\lambda}{\lambda_0} & =\frac{\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right)}{\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right)} \\ & =\frac{5}{36} \times \frac{16}{3}=\frac{20}{27} \\ \therefore \quad \lambda & =\frac{20}{27} \lambda_0 \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{x} & =27 \end{aligned}$$

explanation:
Velocity of electron in the $$\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$$ orbit is

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{nh}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{n}} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}} \end{aligned}$$

Taking the ratio,

$$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{v}_2}{\mathrm{v}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_1}{\mathrm{n}_2}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}_2=\frac{\mathrm{v}_1}{2}$$

Change in velocity,

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta \mathrm{v} & =\left|\mathrm{v}_2-\mathrm{v}_1\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{\mathrm{v}_1}{2}-\mathrm{v}_1\right|=0.5 \mathrm{v}_1 \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, change in percentage is $$50 \%$$

content: In a radioactive disintegration, the ratio of initial number of atoms to the number of atoms present at time $$t=\frac{1}{2 \lambda}$$ is [$$\lambda=$$ decay constant]
explanation:
According to radioactive disintegration law,

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{N}_0}=\mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{N}_0}=\mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \times \frac{1}{2 \lambda}} \quad \ldots\left(\because \mathrm{t}=\frac{1}{2 \lambda}\right) \\ & \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{N}_0}=\mathrm{e}^{-\frac{1}{2}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{N}_0}{\mathrm{~N}}=\mathrm{e}^{\frac{1}{2}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{N}_0}{\mathrm{~N}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{e}} \end{aligned}$$

content: The ratio of the velocity of the electron in the first Bohr orbit to that in the second Bohr orbit of hydrogen atom is
explanation:
Velocity of electron in the $$\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$$ orbit $$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{Ze}^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{nh}}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{n}} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}} \\ & \text { given } \mathrm{n}_1=1 \text { and } \mathrm{n}_2=2, \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{\mathrm{v}_1}{\mathrm{v}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{\mathrm{n}_1}=\frac{2}{1} \end{array}$$

content: The shortest wavelength in the Balmer series of hydrogen atom is equal to the shortest wavelength in the Brackett series of a hydrogen like atom of atomic number $$\mathrm{z}$$. The value of $$\mathrm{z}$$ is
explanation:
Using Rydberg's formula,

$$\frac{1}{\lambda}=R_H Z^2\left[\frac{1}{n^2}-\frac{1}{m^2}\right]$$

where $$R_H$$ is the Rydberg's constant

For calculating the shortest wavelength in the Balmer series of hydrogen atom, $$\mathrm{n}=2, \mathrm{~m}=\infty$$ and $$\mathrm{Z}=1$$

$$\therefore \quad \lambda_1=\frac{4}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}}$$

For hydrogen like atom, the shortest wavelength is given by,

In Brackett series

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{n}=4, \mathrm{~m}=\infty \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}} \cdot \mathrm{Z}^2\left(\frac{1}{16}\right) \\ \therefore \quad \lambda_2 & =\frac{16}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}} \cdot \mathrm{Z}^2} \end{aligned}$$

Given: $$\lambda_1=\lambda_2$$,

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{4}{R_H}=\frac{16}{R_H \cdot Z^2} \Rightarrow Z^2=4 \\ & \therefore \quad Z=2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The ratio of longest to shortest wavelength emitted in Paschen series of hydrogen atom is
explanation:
For Paschen series, Longest wavelength corresponds to

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{n}_1=3, \mathrm{n}_2=\mathrm{n}_1+1=4 \\ & \lambda_{\max }=\frac{144}{7 \mathrm{R}} \end{aligned}$$

Shortest wavelength corresponds to $$\mathrm{n}_1=3, \mathrm{n}_2=\infty$$

$$\begin{aligned} \lambda_{\min } & =\frac{9}{R} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\lambda_{\max }}{\lambda_{\min }} & =\frac{\left(\frac{144}{7 R}\right)}{\left(\frac{9}{R}\right)}=\frac{144}{63} \end{aligned}$$

content: The force acting on the electron in hydrogen atom (Bohr' theory) is related to the principle quantum number '$$n$$' as
explanation:
The centripetal force of the rotating electron is given by $$F=\frac{m v^2}{r}$$

But, according to Bohr,

$$\mathrm{v} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}}$$ and $$\mathrm{r} \propto \mathrm{n}^2$$

i.e. $$F \propto \frac{v^2}{r^2}$$

$$\Rightarrow \mathrm{F} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^2 \mathrm{n}^2}$$

$$F \propto \frac{1}{n^4}$$

content: The wavelength of light for the least energetic photons emitted in the Lyman series of the hydrogen spectrum is nearly [Take $$\mathrm{hc}=1240 ~\mathrm{eV}$$ - $$\mathrm{nm}$$, change in energy of the levels $$=10.2 ~\mathrm{eV}$$ ]
explanation:
For least energetic photon emitted in Lyman series, $$E=E_2-E_1=10.2 \mathrm{~eV}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\mathrm{E}} & =\frac{1240}{10.2} \mathrm{~nm} \\ & =121.57 \mathrm{~nm} \approx 122 \mathrm{~nm} \end{aligned}$$

content: The ratio of wavelengths for transition of electrons from $$2^{\text {nd }}$$ orbit to $$1^{\text {st }}$$ orbit of Helium $$\left(\mathrm{He}^{++}\right)$$ and Lithium $$\left(\mathrm{Li}^{++1}\right)$$ is (Atomic number of Helium $$=2$$, Atomic number of Lithium $$=3$$ )
explanation:
To find the ratio of the wavelengths for the transition of electrons from the $2^{\text{nd}}$ orbit to the $1^{\text{st}}$ orbit for Helium ion $\text{He}^{++}$ and Lithium ion $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$, we can use the formula derived from the Rydberg equation for wavelengths of emitted photons during electron transitions in hydrogen-like atoms.

The Rydberg formula for the wavelength $\lambda$ is given by:

$ \frac{1}{\lambda} = RZ^2 \left( \frac{1}{n_1^2} - \frac{1}{n_2^2} \right) $

where,

  • $R$ is the Rydberg constant for hydrogen,

  • $Z$ is the atomic number of the nucleus,

  • $n_1$ is the lower energy level,

  • $n_2$ is the higher energy level.

For a transition from the $2^{\text{nd}}$ orbit to the $1^{\text{st}}$ orbit ($n_2 = 2$ and $n_1 = 1$), the formula simplifies to:

$ \frac{1}{\lambda} = RZ^2 \left( \frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{2^2} \right) = RZ^2 \left( \frac{3}{4} \right) $

Therefore, $\lambda = \frac{4}{3RZ^2}$.

For Helium ion $\text{He}^{++}$ ($Z = 2$):

$ \lambda_{\text{He}} = \frac{4}{3R \cdot 2^2} = \frac{4}{12R} = \frac{1}{3R} $

For Lithium ion $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ ($Z = 3$):

$ \lambda_{\text{Li}} = \frac{4}{3R \cdot 3^2} = \frac{4}{27R} $

To find the ratio of the wavelengths ($\lambda_{\text{He}} : \lambda_{\text{Li}}$), we divide the expressions obtained for $\lambda_{\text{He}}$ and $\lambda_{\text{Li}}$:

$ \frac{\lambda_{\text{He}}}{\lambda_{\text{Li}}} = \frac{\frac{1}{3R}}{\frac{4}{27R}} = \frac{27}{12} = \frac{9}{4} $

Thus, the ratio of the wavelengths for the transition of electrons from the $2^{\text{nd}}$ orbit to the $1^{\text{st}}$ orbit of Helium ion ($\text{He}^{++}$) to Lithium ion ($\mathrm{Li}^{+}$) is $9 : 4$.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A: $9: 4$.

content: For an electron moving in the $$\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$$ Bohr orbit the deBroglie wavelength of an electron is
explanation:
From de Broglie's hypothesis,

$$\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{n}}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}} \quad \text{.... (i)}$$

and from Bohr's atomic model,

$$\mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}$$

Also,

$$\mathrm{L}=\mathrm{mvr}_{\mathrm{n}}$$

Due to quantization of angular momentum, we can write,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}=\mathrm{mvr}_{\mathrm{n}} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi \mathrm{mr}} \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

putting (ii) into (i), we get,

$$\therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{nh} / 2 \pi \mathrm{mr})}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{n}}$$

content: If an electron in a hydrogen atom jumps from an orbit of level $$n=3$$ to orbit of level $$n=2$$, then the emitted radiation frequency is (where R = Rydberg's constant, C = Velocity of light)
explanation: $$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right] \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{\lambda}=\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right]=\frac{5}{36} \mathrm{R} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\lambda}=\frac{5}{36} \mathrm{Rc} \end{array}$$

content: Using Bohr's model, the orbital period of electron in hydrogen atom in the $$\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$$ orbit is $$\left(\varepsilon_0=\right.$$ permittivity of vacuum, $$\mathrm{h}=$$ Planck's constant, $$\mathrm{m}=$$ mass of electron, $$\mathrm{e}=$$ electronic charge)
explanation:
The period of an electron in the $$\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$$ orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{n}}}{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{n}}} \\ & \text { and } \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{n}^2 \mathrm{~h}^2}{\pi \mathrm{me}^2} \\ & \text { and linear speed } \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{nh}} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{2 \pi \cdot \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{n}^2 \mathrm{~h}^2}{\pi \mathrm{me}^2} \times \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{nh}}{\mathrm{e}^2}=\frac{4 \varepsilon_0^2 \mathrm{n}^3 \mathrm{~h}^3}{\mathrm{me}^4} \end{aligned}$$

content:
The wave number of the last line of the Balmer series in hydrogen spectrum will be

(Rydberg's constant $$=10^7 \mathrm{~m}^{-1}$$ )
explanation:
Wave number $$=\frac{1}{\lambda}=$$ Receiprocal of wavelength

For the last line of the Balmer series, $$n=\infty$$ and the transition is from $$n=\infty$$ to $$n=2$$

$$\therefore$$ Wave number $$\bar{v}=\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{4}$$ and $$\mathrm{R}=10^7 / \mathrm{m}$$

$$\therefore \bar{v}=\frac{R}{4}=\frac{10^7}{4}=\frac{10 \times 10^6}{4}=2.5 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$

content: The half life of a radioactive substance is 30 minute. The time taken between 40% decay and 85% decay of the same radioactive substance is
explanation:
When $$40 \%$$ nuclei decay, $$60 \%$$ nuclei remain undecayed

Let this number be $$\mathrm{N}_0$$

When $$85 \%$$ nuclei decay, $$15 \%$$ nuclei remain undecayed This number will be $$\mathrm{N}=\frac{\mathrm{N}_0}{4}$$

The number of nuclei will become one-fourth in two half lives i.e. 60 minutes.

content: The ratio of energies of photons produced due to transition of electron of hydrogen atom from its (i) second to first energy level and (ii) highest energy level to second energy level is
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{E_1}$$ be the energy of the first energy level Then $$\mathrm{E_2=\frac{E_1}{4}}$$

Highest level $$\mathrm{E_{\infty}}=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad E_2-E_1=\frac{E_1}{4}-E_1=-\frac{3}{4} E_1 \\ & E_{\infty}-E_2=0-\frac{E_1}{4}=-\frac{E_1}{4} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{E_2-E_1}{E_{\infty}-E_2}=3 \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: The ratio of maximum to minimum wavelength in Balmer series of hydrogen atom is
explanation:
Maximum wavelength of Balmer series is given by
